title: Acqua pazza
subtitle: White fish poached in tomato, wine, and olive oil
excerpt: Crazy water: white fish fillets poached in a broth of cherry tomatoes, white wine, garlic, and olive oil. The tomatoes burst in the heat and make a light, aromatic cooking liquor that you spoon over the fish at the table. On the plate in under 30 minutes.
sourceNotes: Acqua pazza is a Neapolitan and Campanian fishing tradition, originally a method used by fishermen to cook freshly caught fish at sea using seawater with a handful of tomatoes and herbs. The name means literally crazy water. Artusi includes Neapolitan fish preparations in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain); the acqua pazza technique is documented throughout southern Italian coastal cooking from the nineteenth century.

content.text: Acqua pazza means crazy water. The name comes from the odd-sounding idea of cooking fish in plain seawater with a few tomatoes and some herbs. The tomatoes burst in the heat and turn the water into something between a broth and a sauce. It is the right thing to serve with a piece of good white fish.

Sea bass, sea bream, and red snapper all work. The fillets should be skin-on and not too thin; a 2 cm fillet will cook in 8 minutes without drying out. The olive oil goes in at two stages: a little at the beginning to soften the garlic, and a generous drizzle of extra-virgin at the very end when the pan is off the heat.

items.name: Cod fillet
items.prepNote: sea bass, sea bream, or red snapper; skin-on, about 180 g each

content.text: Warm the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide, lidded pan over medium heat. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and cook for 1 to 2 minutes until pale gold, do not let it brown. Add the {{cherry-tomatoes}} of cherry tomatoes and cook for 3 to 4 minutes, pressing them gently so they start to burst and release their juice. Add the capers if using. Pour in the {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine and 100 ml of water. Season with salt. Bring to a simmer.

Season the {{cod-fillet}} skin-side with salt. Lower them into the broth skin-side up. Cover and cook over medium-low heat for 7 to 9 minutes, depending on thickness, until the flesh is just opaque and flakes when pressed lightly. Remove from heat. Scatter the {{parsley-flat}} of parsley over, then drizzle with the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of extra-virgin olive oil. Serve directly from the pan, spooning the broth generously over each fillet. Good with crusty bread to mop up the liquid.

items.fix: Keep at a low simmer; test at 7 minutes by pressing the thickest part. It should yield with gentle resistance
items.cause: Cooked too long, or heat too high; white fish fillets need gentle heat

items.fix: Cook the tomatoes until they fully burst before adding the wine; use only 100 ml of water
items.cause: Cherry tomatoes not cooked long enough before adding wine; or too much water added

items.fix: Keep garlic at pale gold, not brown; add the tomatoes as soon as the garlic is fragrant
items.cause: Allowed to brown or burn before the tomatoes were added

items.fix: Add a generous drizzle of good extra-virgin olive oil off the heat; it ties the flavours together
items.cause: Extra-virgin olive oil omitted at the end; finishing oil is not optional

content.text: Acqua pazza started as a fisherman's dish on the islands and coasts of Campania. The Aeolian Islands and the coast between Naples and Salerno are the home ground. Fishermen worked the waters with a small brazier on board. They cooked the catch in seawater with whatever they had: some tomatoes, a handful of herbs, a splash of wine if they had it. The seawater gave salt and mineral depth. The tomatoes gave acid and sweetness. The dish moved ashore into Neapolitan cooking and then through the restaurants of the south. It is still the go-to way to cook fine white fish without overwhelming it.
